instagram.author: thezanelim
instagram.body:
5 Casual Pants Every Man Needs
Here are five casual pants I think every man needs in their wardrobe. The first is a pair of dark denim. Look for one in a slightly higher rise and a slightly wider leg, so you can wear across different functions, whether it is a date night or business casual meeting. The next is actually my favorite of the five and it is a pair of lighter wash denims. These reads more casual than the darker denim, but what I really love about this is its ability to contrast a darker top. The next are these pair of green fatigues. Visually what makes a fatigue stand out is the pockets in the front. It gives it a much more casual and rugged look. Fatigues also have side tabs if you don't want to wear a belt with it, which just gives it a little bit more functionality in that regard. The next is a pair of brown cotton canvas pants. Often times after people already got their denims, they tend to default to a pair of black pants. I actually think a pair of brown pants is actually more versatile and fun comparatively to a black. This is a double knee pant with a very wide opening. If your other pants are already on the slimmer side, it is fun to play around with different silhouettes. Lastly, a pair of cream pants. I actually don't own a pair of cream pants right now, and so for now I'm defaulting to my cream denim trousers. It contrasts colors incredibly well, and like a pair of dark denims, it surprisingly goes well with almost everything in a man's wardrobe. Let me know what you think of these casual pants and if you'll make any adjustments to this collection.

The only pant you need @leset 🤍
One pant, five completely different looks and honestly, that's the whole philosophy. When you find the right piece you don't need more you just need to think differently about what you already have. This is how I approach getting dressed. Less in your closet, more intention behind it. More creativity, more versatility, more getting dressed in a way that actually feels like you. as a closer if your objection handling is just pulling up the first word track that comes to mind that you have memorized you do not know how to objection handle instead your focus should be on actually understanding why these objections happen in the first place so let's dive into it you need to understand that most people will not naturally say yes to them making a decision that requires them to invest into themselves their time their energy into them following through on something so when they're giving you an objection it's normally them not just flat out saying no to you they're normally just trying to work through their own internal doubts they're asking themselves is this actually worth it will I follow through with it will this actually get me to where I wanna go with my life as a closer your role isn't just to push past it and try to get them to say yes because even if they did they will likely have buyers remorse after because they did not fully get to process what they were feeling you your job is to help give them clarity that way they can actually make a decision that they feel confident with it you need to understand that the first objection that someone gives you it usually sounds like it makes sense on the surface but it is almost never the actual objection so your process could tell you oh I need to think about it but deep down they're really just unsure if your product will actually work for them long term the reason why they'll do that is because they don't actually feel comfortable expressing their real doubts and concerns out loud so they'll give you an answer that feels safe and comfortable that allows them to put a pause in the conversation without them actually having to explain themselves and this is why your first move should never be the first thing that pops to your head the first word track that you had memorized because it is not going to work your first move should be to ask questions that way you can actually get an understanding of what's causing this hesitation and obviously this is assuming that you pre handled everything in the conversation during discovery and that even gives you more of an insight is this objection a smoke screen or the actual objection because if you pre handled it and you should know that you guys overcame and worked through it together and that allows you to look at this with a lot more clarity I have a seven step objection handling framework comment down sales if you wanna see how this actually works

How should you handle a customer who says, “I’m just looking”?   It is one of the most common phrases in the showroom, and often the most misunderstood. “I’m just looking” is not a rejection. It is a natural, reflexive response that needs the right approach.   ✅ Stay calm and avoid getting defensive ✅ Remember people do not visit a dealership by accident ✅ Take the pressure off by acknowledging they are just browsing ✅ Ask permission to show vehicles while they browse ✅ Gently explore interest with questions about specials or specific models ✅ Work with the customer for 15 to 20 minutes before making a buying judgement   When customers feel relaxed rather than pressured, they open up. That is when real conversations start and buying intent becomes clear.   Celebrating 20 Years of TSS 🏆 www.totalsellingsolutions.com.au   #TotalSellingSolutions #TSS #20YearsOfTSS #AutomotiveSales #SalesTraining #AutomotiveIndustry
How should you handle a customer that says I'm just looking firstly, you gotta remember that when you go into a store and a salesperson approaches you ask yourself how many times do you say those sales people I'm just looking when you think about it it's a natural human reflective answer to give as a salesperson can reduce the amount of times you get I'm just looking by walking up the customer and say hi, I'm Steve and you are great nice to meet you I noticed you're looking at the XYZ car you're then looking at that particular a car first of all you gotta start connecting with them so if they say to you, oh look here I just wanna get some pricing on it or they might say look I'm just having a look around today first thing is and I fall into this, don't get defensive think about why someone got up this morning and driven to your car yard and started to have a look around in your yard alright, they're in the market to by car they're getting defensive straight away it's a natural reflexive answer back to you they're gonna say I'm just looking so the worst thing you can do is defensive so what I would do is I'll turn around and I'll just take the pressure straight off and say that's alright, so today's a looking day it's all good that's where it all starts can I show you some Vehicles while you're having a look around and you might start warming them up to the notion of some pricing. So would you look at something special if we had some cars for you that were on special? Or did you just wanna look at a particular car? So an overall type of situation. So they might go, what have you got on special? So if it's a customer that's pretty hot and want to change their car over, or if they back off again and get, no, we just wanna have a look around today, then you need to back off as well and work with those customers. Whatever you do, you need to be working with customers or 15 or 20 minutes before you make a judgment, is this customer here to buy a car today? Ask yourself, why are people in your car yard today?

youtube.body:
6 Women's Linen Brands You MUST Stop Buying in 2026 (And 3 You Can Actually Trust)
There's a Talbots linen popover hangingin your closet right now that you bought 8 years ago. It still fits. The colorstill holds. The seams are still clean. Now, go to Talbots today. Pick up whatlooks like the same popover and wash it three times. The stitching pulls. Thecolor fades. [music] The linen feels like it lost half itsweight. Same name on the tag. Completelydifferent product inside it. That is the state of women's linenclothing in 2026. The prices are real. The brand names arereal. But the linen is not what it used to be,and most women have not noticed because the tags still say the same names theytrusted 10 years [music] ago. Three of these six brands are nowsecretly controlled by the exact same company. Same owners, same cost-cuttingplaybook, same declining quality. Different logo on the tag.We are going to break down all six. And then we are going to give you threebrands that are actually worth your money. Including one that uses the sameEuropean flax mills as $200 labels, but charges a fraction of that price.But first, a quick word on what real linen actually is, because that is wherethe scam starts. Linen comes from flax, a plant grownprimarily in France, Belgium, and Lithuania.The best linen clothing uses 100% European flax fiber at a fabric weightbetween 180 and 260 g per square meter. That weight gives you structure withoutstiffness. It breathes in heat. It softens with every wash. And it lastsfor years. The expensive label does not make thefiber better. The fiber is the fiber. But what most brands sell as linen in2026 is something different. It is a linen blend. Sometimes 50% linen and 50%polyester. Sometimes 70% viscose with a linen touch. The tag might say linen.The product listing might say linen. But the fiber composition, if you readthe fine print on the care label, tells a different story.Here is a simple rule. If a linen garment feels smooth and softstraight out of the packaging, it is probably not pure linen.Real linen has a slight crispness when it is new.That crispness becomes softness over time.A blend feels soft immediately because the polyester or rayon is doing thework. And polyester does not get better withage. It gets worse. Now let us get into the brands. And weare starting with the three that are going to hurt the most.Number one, Talbots. If you have been shopping at Talbots for20 or 30 years, this one is going to sting.Talbots used to make linen that you handed down. Their popovers were asummer uniform for an entire generation of professional women. Structured enoughfor the office, soft enough for Saturday.That Talbots does not exist anymore. In 2012, a private equity firm calledSycamore Partners bought Talbots for $391 million.The company had been struggling. Five straight years of declining sales,a failed attempt to attract younger customers that alienated the loyal base.Sycamore came in with a turnaround plan. And here is what turnaround looks likein private equity. You keep the brand name, you keep theprice point. But you change the suppliers, you thinthe fabrics, and you reduce the quality controls.The woman shopping at Talbots in 2026 is paying the same price or more for aproduct that would not have passed Talbots own standards 15 years ago.The reviews tell the story. Customers with decades of designeducation calling the stitching on current Talbots linen dreadful.Misaligned chest pockets. Seems that would not pass an eighthgrade sewing class. Linen that fades after two washes when the old linen heldits color for years. The label remembers who it was. Thefabric does not. But Sycamore Partners was not finishedwith just Talbots. Remember that name? Number two, J. Jill.Here is the cruelest irony in women's fashion right now.J. Jill was built on linen. It was the founding identity of the brand.Artistically inspired, beautifully dyed, textured linen pieces designed for womenwho wanted comfort with personality. Your wardrobe was 80% J. Jill, and youwere proud of that. Today, J. Jill sells more linen thanever. And the quality is lower than ever.Longtime customers, women who have shopped there for 25 years, are writingreviews that read like breakup letters. Knockout colors on the cheapest linen.Fading after one wash. Poorly sewn seams. And a new design direction thatis pushing toward younger demographics with cropped lengths and trendy cutsthat their core customer, the woman over 55, did not ask for and does not want.One customer review put it perfectly. She said they sell belly button shirtsnow. And mature women do not wear bellybutton shirts. J. Jill's problem is structural.To maintain their release schedule, full new linen collections every few months,they cannot source the quality of flax that made them famous.Volume demands cheaper supply. Cheaper supply means thinner fabric,weaker dye adhesion, and inconsistent weave density.The brand grew too fast for its own standards.They did not change direction. They changed customer.But the next brand is where all of this connects. Because the company thatbought Chico's is the same company that is already running Talbots.Number three, Chico's. And here is where the connecting threadcomes together. In January 2024, Sycamore Partners, thesame private equity firm that bought Talbots, acquired Chico's FAS.That includes Chico's, White House Black Market, and Soma.They merged everything under a holding company called Knitwell Group. So nowlet us count what Sycamore Partners controls. Talbots, Ann Taylor, Loft,Lane Bryant, Chico's, White House Black Market, Soma.All under one roof. A $6 billion fashion conglomerate that most women have neverheard of. The pattern is predictable.When one company controls this many brands, they consolidate suppliers. Theymerge production lines. They share fabrics across labels. The corporatelanguage calls it economies of scale. What it actually means is that the linenin your Chico's blouse and the linen in your Talbots popover might be comingfrom the same factory at the same reduced quality with a different labelsewn in at the end. Chico's customers are already noticing.Prices jumped. Selection narrowed. The linen pieces that used to hold theirshape through a full summer of wear are now losing structure after a few weeks.The brand still charges $100 for a linen tunic.But the tunic is not what it was. Those are not separate brands anymore.That is one company wearing six different outfits.So far we covered three linen brands to avoid. The full guide covers 85 clothingand shoe brands scored and ranked with a quality check method you can use in anystore in 60 seconds. Every linen brand in this video is inthere. Plus dozens more you have not heard me talk about yet.The link is right below in the description or scan the QR code that'son screen right now. Now, the next three brands are not onesyou probably have in your closet, but they are traps that catch a lot of womenshopping online or on vacation. Number four, Zara.Walk into any Zara store in July and the linen section looks stunning.Beautiful cuts, trending silhouettes, neutral colorways that photographperfectly. The price feels reasonable. A linenblouse for $50, a pair of wide-leg linen trousers for $60.But look at the care label. Most Zara linen pieces are linen blends.Significant amounts of viscose, polyester, or cotton are mixed in toreduce production costs and speed up manufacturing. Zara's entire supplychain is built for speed. The company can go from design to storeshelf in 2 weeks. Real linen does not work on that timeline. Quality linenneeds to be stone-washed and softened during production, a process that takestime Zara simply does not have. The estimated manufacturing cost of aZara linen blouse at this fabric weight runs $8 to $12.At $50 retail, that is a five to six times markup, which is actually lowerthan the industry average. But after five washes, the blend loses shape. Theviscose component starts to sag in areas where pure linen would hold.The color fades unevenly because different fibers absorb dye differently.Zara designs clothes to look incredible for one season.If you understand that and treat it as disposable fashion, the price is fair.But if you are buying Zara linen thinking it will become a summer stapleyou reach for year after year, you are paying for a promise the fabric cannotkeep. Number five, H&M.H&M has done something clever with their marketing.They created a conscious collection and a premium selection that signalssustainability and quality. The words organic and natural appeareverywhere. The hang tags mention responsible sourcing.But the linen pieces in these collections are still blends.And here is where H&M actually deserves a small amount of credit. Their pricingis more honest about it. A linen blend top for 20 to $30 does notpretend to be an investment piece. The problem is the expectation gap.Women over 50 who grew up with J. Crew linen and Lands' End linen see H&M linenon a vacation shopping trip and assume it performs the same way. It does not.After washing, H&M linen blends shrink more aggressively than pure linen.The color loss is noticeable after just three cycles.And the pilling on blended areas, especially around the underarms andwhere a handbag strap rests, shows up within weeks.H&M linen is acceptable if you need a beach cover-up for a long weekend. It isnot acceptable if you are building a summer wardrobe that needs to last untilSeptember. Number six, Shein and Temu.This one should be obvious, but it needs to be said because the deception here isthe most brazen. Shein and Temu list thousands of itemswith the word linen in the title. Linen dress, linen blouse, linen wide-legpants, $12, $15. Read the fabric composition.Most of these items are polyester with a linen texture finish.Or they are a 15/85 blend where the 15% linen is decoration, not function.The fabric does not breathe like linen, does not soften like linen, and does notlast like linen. The real damage is not the $12 you lost.The real damage is what it does to your expectations.Women buy Shein linen, find it scratchy and shapeless, and conclude that linenis an overhyped fabric. They never actually wore linen. Theywore polyester cos playing as linen. And that is a loss because real linen isextraordinary. You just have to know where to find it.So, here are three brands that are actually worth your money.And the first one costs less than you think.Number one, Quince. This is the brand that makes the entirelinen industry look ridiculous. Quince sells 100% European linen.Shirts for $30, trousers for 40, dresses for 50. Same flax, same mills, samefiber quality as brands charging 150 to $300.How? No retail stores, no wholesalemiddlemen, no celebrity partnerships, no glossy magazine campaigns.They work directly with factories and manage their own supply chain. You payfor the fabric and the labor, not the billboard on 5th Avenue.If you watched our Costco video, you already know how this works. Samefactory, fraction of [music] the price. Their linen is OEKO-TEX certified, whichmeans it has been tested for harmful substances at every stage of production.Customer reviews consistently compare the hand feel and drape to Eileen Fisherat a third of the price. The only downside is you cannot try it on in astore. But Quince offers free returns,>> [music] >> so the risk is a trip to the postoffice, not a hit to your bank account. You are not paying for the linen atother brands, you are paying for the logo on the label.Quince skips the logo and gives you the linen.Number two, Eileen Fisher. This is the premium answer and it isworth every dollar if you buy it strategically.Eileen Fisher uses 100% organic linen, dyed to bluesign standards withouthazardous chemicals, and certified as a B corporation.Their pieces are designed to last 10 to 15 years, not because of marketing,because the fiber and construction actually support that lifespan.Women who wear Eileen Fisher linen talk about it the way other people talk abouta good mattress. Once you find the right one, you stoplooking. The fabric softens with every washinstead of degrading. The cuts are simple enough that they donot date themselves. A linen shift you buy this summer looksexactly as current five summers from now.The retail prices run $128 to $328 per piece. That sounds steep, but here isthe strategy. Buy Eileen Fisher through NordstromRack, Poshmark, ThredUp, or their own sale section.A blouse that retailed for $228 appears on resale for $75 to $90.And it has years of life left in it because the linen was built to endure.Eileen Fisher also runs a renew program where you return worn pieces for storecredit. youtube.body:
Why 90% of Women Don’t Look Good in Linen — How Elegant Women FIX IT
Let's be honest, linen sounds great, breathable, light, effortless until you put it on. Suddenly is wrinkled, shapeless, and somehow makes you look like it got dressed in a hurry. And no, the fix isn't to obsess over steaming or trying to keep it wrinkle-free, because linen is supposed to wrinkle. The trick is knowing how to wear it. So the wrinkles feel intentional and not sloppy. Once you get this right, linen becomes one of the most elegant, relaxed fabrics you will ever wear. Let me show you how. The problem isn't linen, it's the way we wear it. Linen on its own doesn't flatter everyone right out of the box. It's loose. It wrinkles. It often comes with elastic waistbands. On paper, it's not exactly a style icon, but when you learn to work with its natural qualities instead of fighting them, it can look incredibly chic. So, what goes wrong? The biggest issue is mismatch. Wearing linen in an outfit that's otherwise sharp, sleek, and wrinkle-free sets the wrong tone. It makes your outfit feel unfinished, not effortless. Let's say you're wearing a crisp, structured vest with tailored lines and a pair of wrinkled linen pants, it just feels off. But if you swap that same vest for something softer, like a linen blend top with a relaxed silhouette, suddenly the outfit makes sense. You're learning into the relaxed nature of linen instead of resisting it. Linen can look polished. Here's how. There is a myth that linen is only for beach days or lazy weekends, but that's not true. But to make it feel refined, you need to build the rest of your look with intention. Start with the right shape. A linen trouser with clean lines and an high waist can looks just as elegant as tailored pants in wool or cotton as long as the fabric is thick enough to hold shape. Fit is crucial. Baggy or saggy linen will never look elevated. If your pants come with a drawstring or elastic waistband, you can balance that out with a more fitted top or a belt that defines your waist. Belts are your best friend when styling linen. A simple leather or raphia belt add structure and creates a visual break. Especially helpful when your outfit might otherwise feel a bit too relaxed. And then there's the neckline. Linen pants with a basic tank top can feel too casual, but a subtle change like a square neck, halter neckline, or cap sleeve adds visual interest without sacrificing comfort. The one piece shortcut. If you're ever in doubt, go for a linen dress or jumpsuit. One piece means pure changes to mismatch your outfit and it creates a longer, more flattering line. But here's the secret, the waist. A linen dress that goes straight down does no favors for your shape. Whether you're petite or tall, you will always look better in a dress that cinches at the waist. Empire waists work beautifully, especially on smaller frames. Wrap dresses, tie waist, or even belted shirt dresses create that essential shape that makes linen feel polished. Want to take it a step further? Choose linen in a darker color like navy, chocolate, or dark gray. These shades automatically dress up the fabric and make the wrinkles look softer, not sloppy. How to dress up linen for evening. Yes, linen can absolutely be worn at night, but it takes finesse. You need balance. If your pants are linen, don't pair them with a satin top and sparkly heels. The contrast is too sharp. Instead, try a cotton silk blend top or something with texture that feels line with the linen vibe. Accessories matter even more. A narrow metallic belt, a structured pouch, an elegant sandals can shift linen into evening territory. Look for black or nude leather, soft gold or silver details, and shoes that show some skin like strappy sandals instead of sneakers or pumps. The wrong shoes can ruin the look. Sneakers, mules, and pumps often don't play well with linen. Sneakers, even in white, add weight at the base and stop the visual flow of lightweight linen pants. Mules cover too much of your foot and look bottom heavy and pumps too corporate. They fight the breezy energy of linen. Instead, go for shoes that are light in color and shape. slides, strappy sandals, open toe flats. All of these keep the look balanced. New tones or soft neutrals like tan and beige work especially well. Linen and body shape. Finding your fit. One of the reasons linen feels unflattering is because we treat like it will do the work for us. But with the right cuts and styling tricks, it can absolutely work for any body type. If you're petite, lean into vertical lines. Go for linen pieces that create one long visual column like jumpsuits, wrap dresses, or high-waisted trousers with a fitted top. Avoid oversized shapes unless they're balanced by a cinched waist or structured accessory. If you're curvy, seek out linen with seams, panels, or darts. These give shape to a fabric that otherwise wants to hang straight. Emphasize your waist, balance proportions, and don't be afraid to tailor if something almost fits. If you're tall, you've got room to play. Longer hems, wide leg cuts, dramatic sleeves. Linen responds well to length and movement. Just be sure the fit is intentional so it doesn't slide into wrinkled sack territory. Fabric blends and smarter shopping. Not all linen is created equal. 100% linen is breezy but often stiff and quick to wrinkle. Linen cotton blends hold their shape a little bit better. Linen visos can give you a softer drape. If you want less fuss, look for those blends. And especially if you want your pieces to last longer and wrinkle less. Also, check the thickness. Flimsy linen clings and creases in all the wrong ways. Go for mediumwe linen that holds its shape but still let your skin breathe. Linen isn't a problem. It's our expectations. If you want it to behave like structured wool or polished satin, it won't. But if you meet it where it is and style it accordingly, linen can be one of the most flattering and versatile fabrics in your wardrobe. Let the wrinkles be part of the story, not the whole story. So, next time you reach for a linen piece, don't try to dress it up with stiff, sharp items. Stay cohesive. Keep the look soft, styled, and strategic. And if you want more tips on styling tricky pieces and make them work for your body, make sure to grab the style guide linked below. It's full of practical swabs and refined solutions. Don't forget to subscribe to my channel and thank you for watching.

source_items.author: {'id': 'UCo6uFyf_KvnqA9X3PrnQquQ', 'title': 'Her Style Audit', 'handle': 'herstyleaudit', 'thumbnail': 'https://yt3.ggpht.com/nXFZMoF9Y92xPa6qeYCGiMEa10A6kUf8AYOJjXk225xZ8paneZ_X54ndF4bB4uR0ew_f_GZW=s68-c-k-c0x00ffffff-no-rj'}
source_items.body:
6 Women's Linen Brands You MUST Stop Buying in 2026 (And 3 You Can Actually Trust)
There's a Talbots linen popover hangingin your closet right now that you bought 8 years ago. It still fits. The colorstill holds. The seams are still clean. Now, go to Talbots today. Pick up whatlooks like the same popover and wash it three times. The stitching pulls. Thecolor fades. [music] The linen feels like it lost half itsweight. Same name on the tag. Completelydifferent product inside it. That is the state of women's linenclothing in 2026. The prices are real. The brand names arereal. But the linen is not what it used to be,and most women have not noticed because the tags still say the same names theytrusted 10 years [music] ago. Three of these six brands are nowsecretly controlled by the exact same company. Same owners, same cost-cuttingplaybook, same declining quality. Different logo on the tag.We are going to break down all six. And then we are going to give you threebrands that are actually worth your money. Including one that uses the sameEuropean flax mills as $200 labels, but charges a fraction of that price.But first, a quick word on what real linen actually is, because that is wherethe scam starts. Linen comes from flax, a plant grownprimarily in France, Belgium, and Lithuania.The best linen clothing uses 100% European flax fiber at a fabric weightbetween 180 and 260 g per square meter. That weight gives you structure withoutstiffness. It breathes in heat. It softens with every wash. And it lastsfor years. The expensive label does not make thefiber better. The fiber is the fiber. But what most brands sell as linen in2026 is something different. It is a linen blend. Sometimes 50% linen and 50%polyester. Sometimes 70% viscose with a linen touch. The tag might say linen.The product listing might say linen. But the fiber composition, if you readthe fine print on the care label, tells a different story.Here is a simple rule. If a linen garment feels smooth and softstraight out of the packaging, it is probably not pure linen.Real linen has a slight crispness when it is new.That crispness becomes softness over time.A blend feels soft immediately because the polyester or rayon is doing thework. And polyester does not get better withage. It gets worse. Now let us get into the brands. And weare starting with the three that are going to hurt the most.Number one, Talbots. If you have been shopping at Talbots for20 or 30 years, this one is going to sting.Talbots used to make linen that you handed down. Their popovers were asummer uniform for an entire generation of professional women. Structured enoughfor the office, soft enough for Saturday.That Talbots does not exist anymore. In 2012, a private equity firm calledSycamore Partners bought Talbots for $391 million.The company had been struggling. Five straight years of declining sales,a failed attempt to attract younger customers that alienated the loyal base.Sycamore came in with a turnaround plan. And here is what turnaround looks likein private equity. You keep the brand name, you keep theprice point. But you change the suppliers, you thinthe fabrics, and you reduce the quality controls.The woman shopping at Talbots in 2026 is paying the same price or more for aproduct that would not have passed Talbots own standards 15 years ago.The reviews tell the story. Customers with decades of designeducation calling the stitching on current Talbots linen dreadful.Misaligned chest pockets. Seems that would not pass an eighthgrade sewing class. Linen that fades after two washes when the old linen heldits color for years. The label remembers who it was. Thefabric does not. But Sycamore Partners was not finishedwith just Talbots. Remember that name? Number two, J. Jill.Here is the cruelest irony in women's fashion right now.J. Jill was built on linen. It was the founding identity of the brand.Artistically inspired, beautifully dyed, textured linen pieces designed for womenwho wanted comfort with personality. Your wardrobe was 80% J. Jill, and youwere proud of that. Today, J. Jill sells more linen thanever. And the quality is lower than ever.Longtime customers, women who have shopped there for 25 years, are writingreviews that read like breakup letters. Knockout colors on the cheapest linen.Fading after one wash. Poorly sewn seams. And a new design direction thatis pushing toward younger demographics with cropped lengths and trendy cutsthat their core customer, the woman over 55, did not ask for and does not want.One customer review put it perfectly. She said they sell belly button shirtsnow. And mature women do not wear bellybutton shirts. J. Jill's problem is structural.To maintain their release schedule, full new linen collections every few months,they cannot source the quality of flax that made them famous.Volume demands cheaper supply. Cheaper supply means thinner fabric,weaker dye adhesion, and inconsistent weave density.The brand grew too fast for its own standards.They did not change direction. They changed customer.But the next brand is where all of this connects. Because the company thatbought Chico's is the same company that is already running Talbots.Number three, Chico's. And here is where the connecting threadcomes together. In January 2024, Sycamore Partners, thesame private equity firm that bought Talbots, acquired Chico's FAS.That includes Chico's, White House Black Market, and Soma.They merged everything under a holding company called Knitwell Group. So nowlet us count what Sycamore Partners controls. Talbots, Ann Taylor, Loft,Lane Bryant, Chico's, White House Black Market, Soma.All under one roof. A $6 billion fashion conglomerate that most women have neverheard of. The pattern is predictable.When one company controls this many brands, they consolidate suppliers. Theymerge production lines. They share fabrics across labels. The corporatelanguage calls it economies of scale. What it actually means is that the linenin your Chico's blouse and the linen in your Talbots popover might be comingfrom the same factory at the same reduced quality with a different labelsewn in at the end. Chico's customers are already noticing.Prices jumped. Selection narrowed. The linen pieces that used to hold theirshape through a full summer of wear are now losing structure after a few weeks.The brand still charges $100 for a linen tunic.But the tunic is not what it was. Those are not separate brands anymore.That is one company wearing six different outfits.So far we covered three linen brands to avoid. The full guide covers 85 clothingand shoe brands scored and ranked with a quality check method you can use in anystore in 60 seconds. Every linen brand in this video is inthere. Plus dozens more you have not heard me talk about yet.The link is right below in the description or scan the QR code that'son screen right now. Now, the next three brands are not onesyou probably have in your closet, but they are traps that catch a lot of womenshopping online or on vacation. Number four, Zara.Walk into any Zara store in July and the linen section looks stunning.Beautiful cuts, trending silhouettes, neutral colorways that photographperfectly. The price feels reasonable. A linenblouse for $50, a pair of wide-leg linen trousers for $60.But look at the care label. Most Zara linen pieces are linen blends.Significant amounts of viscose, polyester, or cotton are mixed in toreduce production costs and speed up manufacturing. Zara's entire supplychain is built for speed. The company can go from design to storeshelf in 2 weeks. Real linen does not work on that timeline. Quality linenneeds to be stone-washed and softened during production, a process that takestime Zara simply does not have. The estimated manufacturing cost of aZara linen blouse at this fabric weight runs $8 to $12.At $50 retail, that is a five to six times markup, which is actually lowerthan the industry average. But after five washes, the blend loses shape. Theviscose component starts to sag in areas where pure linen would hold.The color fades unevenly because different fibers absorb dye differently.Zara designs clothes to look incredible for one season.If you understand that and treat it as disposable fashion, the price is fair.But if you are buying Zara linen thinking it will become a summer stapleyou reach for year after year, you are paying for a promise the fabric cannotkeep. Number five, H&M.H&M has done something clever with their marketing.They created a conscious collection and a premium selection that signalssustainability and quality. The words organic and natural appeareverywhere. The hang tags mention responsible sourcing.But the linen pieces in these collections are still blends.And here is where H&M actually deserves a small amount of credit. Their pricingis more honest about it. A linen blend top for 20 to $30 does notpretend to be an investment piece. The problem is the expectation gap.Women over 50 who grew up with J. Crew linen and Lands' End linen see H&M linenon a vacation shopping trip and assume it performs the same way. It does not.After washing, H&M linen blends shrink more aggressively than pure linen.The color loss is noticeable after just three cycles.And the pilling on blended areas, especially around the underarms andwhere a handbag strap rests, shows up within weeks.H&M linen is acceptable if you need a beach cover-up for a long weekend. It isnot acceptable if you are building a summer wardrobe that needs to last untilSeptember. Number six, Shein and Temu.This one should be obvious, but it needs to be said because the deception here isthe most brazen. Shein and Temu list thousands of itemswith the word linen in the title. Linen dress, linen blouse, linen wide-legpants, $12, $15. Read the fabric composition.Most of these items are polyester with a linen texture finish.Or they are a 15/85 blend where the 15% linen is decoration, not function.The fabric does not breathe like linen, does not soften like linen, and does notlast like linen. The real damage is not the $12 you lost.The real damage is what it does to your expectations.Women buy Shein linen, find it scratchy and shapeless, and conclude that linenis an overhyped fabric. They never actually wore linen. Theywore polyester cos playing as linen. And that is a loss because real linen isextraordinary. You just have to know where to find it.So, here are three brands that are actually worth your money.And the first one costs less than you think.Number one, Quince. This is the brand that makes the entirelinen industry look ridiculous. Quince sells 100% European linen.Shirts for $30, trousers for 40, dresses for 50. Same flax, same mills, samefiber quality as brands charging 150 to $300.How? No retail stores, no wholesalemiddlemen, no celebrity partnerships, no glossy magazine campaigns.They work directly with factories and manage their own supply chain. You payfor the fabric and the labor, not the billboard on 5th Avenue.If you watched our Costco video, you already know how this works. Samefactory, fraction of [music] the price. Their linen is OEKO-TEX certified, whichmeans it has been tested for harmful substances at every stage of production.Customer reviews consistently compare the hand feel and drape to Eileen Fisherat a third of the price. The only downside is you cannot try it on in astore. But Quince offers free returns,>> [music] >> so the risk is a trip to the postoffice, not a hit to your bank account. You are not paying for the linen atother brands, you are paying for the logo on the label.Quince skips the logo and gives you the linen.Number two, Eileen Fisher. This is the premium answer and it isworth every dollar if you buy it strategically.Eileen Fisher uses 100% organic linen, dyed to bluesign standards withouthazardous chemicals, and certified as a B corporation.Their pieces are designed to last 10 to 15 years, not because of marketing,because the fiber and construction actually support that lifespan.Women who wear Eileen Fisher linen talk about it the way other people talk abouta good mattress. Once you find the right one, you stoplooking. The fabric softens with every washinstead of degrading. The cuts are simple enough that they donot date themselves. A linen shift you buy this summer looksexactly as current five summers from now.The retail prices run $128 to $328 per piece. That sounds steep, but here isthe strategy. Buy Eileen Fisher through NordstromRack, Poshmark, ThredUp, or their own sale section.A blouse that retailed for $228 appears on resale for $75 to $90.And it has years of life left in it because the linen was built to endure.Eileen Fisher also runs a renew program where you return worn pieces for storecredit. They clean them, repair them, and resell them.A garment that lasts long enough to be resold twice is not an expense. It is aninvestment that pays for itself. A good linen piece does not need to bereplaced. It only needs to be washed. Number three, Everlane.Everlane sits in the sweet spot between Quince's value pricing and EileenFisher's premium quality. Their linen collection runs $78 to $198.Blazers, trousers, button-downs, and dresses designed for professionalsettings where you need to look polished but cannot afford to sweat through silkin August. What makes Everlane different from everybrand on the avoid list is transparency. They publish the production cost ofevery single garment on their website. You can see exactly what the rawmaterials cost, what the labor cost, what the transport cost, and whatEverlane's markup is. No other linen brand in this price range does that.The fabric weight is heavier than Zara or H&M, but lighter than Eileen Fisher.That middle ground is the sweet spot for daily wear.It holds up through machine washing without the babying that some premiumlinen demands. And the sizing is consistent enough that repeat orderingworks without trying things on. Here is what the three brands you cantrust have in common. They sell you fabric, not a brand experience. And hereis what the six brands you should stop buying have in common. They are all moreinterested in their label than in their linen.The markup on women's linen in 2026 averages eight to 10 times productioncost. Three of the six brands we covered are owned by the same private equityfirm. And the quality at every single one ofthem has declined while the prices have gone up.Now I want to hear from you. What is the oldest linen piece still inyour closet and what brand made it? Let us know in the comments. Cuz I havea feeling most of those answers are going to be brands that no longer makelinen like that.

source_items.body:
9 Most Flattering Ways to Wear Pants if You are Short
If you're short like me, I'm 5'2, you may have noticed pants and trousers can make you look even shorter because if you don't do it the right way, they can highlight the fact that your legs are short. But if you get it right, they also become your best friends and even make you look stunning. So today, let me share with you the nine very specific outfit combinations that can automatically make you look taller. Make sure you stay till the end. I'm going to talk about casual pants with flat shoes. Oh, these are the hardest. So, make sure you don't miss this part. Everything is linked down below and tagged on YouTube shopping or scan the QR code to go to my blog post. Starting with something we all have, black dress pants. I got it from Spanx. These are petite size kick flare shape, which I know is supposed to elongate our legs. One of the most flattering leg shapes. However, if I wear it like this, which a lot of us do and I used to as well, even though it doesn't look bad, but it's just not that flattering. First thing I'm going to do is to change to a different undershirt, a white crew neck tea. Simple step, but make your outfit look more up to-date. Then the jacket is the main thing. Instead of a regular size blazer, these are actually not that long. They're regular size, but I'm a shorter person, they look really long and make your legs look cut off. So, I'm switching to a crop jacket. This one is from Abberroi. It's already looking better. But these shoes got to go because these pants are 27 inch inseam and my ankle length is only 25. So these are already underneath my ankle. This is not the right length to wear with flat shoes, especially not high vamp shoes like both shoes. Switching to these pair from Sam Edelman. These are only 2 in. Very easy to walk in, but immediately your bottom half look streamlined. There's no more bunching up of fabrics and therefore look taller. work pants normally is a big struggle for those of us with shorter legs. Before I show you how you can actually look good in them, let me first thank Quins for sponsoring today's video. I love their stuff. Fabrics are always high quality, but the price point is so affordable. Crepe W pants, one of my favorites to wear for work. They make three different lengths. I'm wearing the 28 in inseam plated. If you carry more weight in your midsection like me, these are so flattering. I got this in two different colors, the moral gray and the ivory. For the ivory, I love to wear in a monochromatic outfit because I got a matching set. This is a vest. This looks so expensive. Looks like I paid hundreds. For business casual, I love to pair it with brushed cashmere sweater. Slightly shorter. It's nice and thick, but it's also buttery soft. For a desk to dinner look, I like to pair them with this sweater tank top. Organic cotton looks very designer because of the waffle knit. Nude strappy heels make me look better in any of my pants outfit. So scan the QR code or go to the link in my description box. Trust me, I've tried a lot. These are the most leg elongating pairings. You got to get them. Next, let's talk about cropped pants. Raise your hand if you're still thinking they're the easiest to wear. I used to think so. source_items.body:
How should you handle a customer who says, “I’m just looking”?   It is one of the most common phrases in the showroom, and often the most misunderstood. “I’m just looking” is not a rejection. It is a natural, reflexive response that needs the right approach.   ✅ Stay calm and avoid getting defensive ✅ Remember people do not visit a dealership by accident ✅ Take the pressure off by acknowledging they are just browsing ✅ Ask permission to show vehicles while they browse ✅ Gently explore interest with questions about specials or specific models ✅ Work with the customer for 15 to 20 minutes before making a buying judgement   When customers feel relaxed rather than pressured, they open up. That is when real conversations start and buying intent becomes clear.   Celebrating 20 Years of TSS 🏆 www.totalsellingsolutions.com.au   #TotalSellingSolutions #TSS #20YearsOfTSS #AutomotiveSales #SalesTraining #AutomotiveIndustry

source_items.body:
Why 90% of Women Don’t Look Good in Linen — How Elegant Women FIX IT
Let's be honest, linen sounds great, breathable, light, effortless until you put it on. Suddenly is wrinkled, shapeless, and somehow makes you look like it got dressed in a hurry. And no, the fix isn't to obsess over steaming or trying to keep it wrinkle-free, because linen is supposed to wrinkle. The trick is knowing how to wear it. So the wrinkles feel intentional and not sloppy. Once you get this right, linen becomes one of the most elegant, relaxed fabrics you will ever wear. Let me show you how. The problem isn't linen, it's the way we wear it. Linen on its own doesn't flatter everyone right out of the box. It's loose. It wrinkles. It often comes with elastic waistbands. On paper, it's not exactly a style icon, but when you learn to work with its natural qualities instead of fighting them, it can look incredibly chic. So, what goes wrong? The biggest issue is mismatch. Wearing linen in an outfit that's otherwise sharp, sleek, and wrinkle-free sets the wrong tone. It makes your outfit feel unfinished, not effortless. Let's say you're wearing a crisp, structured vest with tailored lines and a pair of wrinkled linen pants, it just feels off. But if you swap that same vest for something softer, like a linen blend top with a relaxed silhouette, suddenly the outfit makes sense. You're learning into the relaxed nature of linen instead of resisting it. Linen can look polished. Here's how. There is a myth that linen is only for beach days or lazy weekends, but that's not true. But to make it feel refined, you need to build the rest of your look with intention. Start with the right shape. A linen trouser with clean lines and an high waist can looks just as elegant as tailored pants in wool or cotton as long as the fabric is thick enough to hold shape. Fit is crucial. Baggy or saggy linen will never look elevated. If your pants come with a drawstring or elastic waistband, you can balance that out with a more fitted top or a belt that defines your waist. Belts are your best friend when styling linen. A simple leather or raphia belt add structure and creates a visual break. Especially helpful when your outfit might otherwise feel a bit too relaxed. And then there's the neckline. Linen pants with a basic tank top can feel too casual, but a subtle change like a square neck, halter neckline, or cap sleeve adds visual interest without sacrificing comfort. The one piece shortcut. If you're ever in doubt, go for a linen dress or jumpsuit. One piece means pure changes to mismatch your outfit and it creates a longer, more flattering line. But here's the secret, the waist. A linen dress that goes straight down does no favors for your shape. Whether you're petite or tall, you will always look better in a dress that cinches at the waist. Empire waists work beautifully, especially on smaller frames. Wrap dresses, tie waist, or even belted shirt dresses create that essential shape that makes linen feel polished. Want to take it a step further? Choose linen in a darker color like navy, chocolate, or dark gray. These shades automatically dress up the fabric and make the wrinkles look softer, not sloppy. How to dress up linen for evening. Yes, linen can absolutely be worn at night, but it takes finesse. You need balance. If your pants are linen, don't pair them with a satin top and sparkly heels. The contrast is too sharp. Instead, try a cotton silk blend top or something with texture that feels line with the linen vibe. Accessories matter even more. A narrow metallic belt, a structured pouch, an elegant sandals can shift linen into evening territory. Look for black or nude leather, soft gold or silver details, and shoes that show some skin like strappy sandals instead of sneakers or pumps. The wrong shoes can ruin the look. Sneakers, mules, and pumps often don't play well with linen. Sneakers, even in white, add weight at the base and stop the visual flow of lightweight linen pants. Mules cover too much of your foot and look bottom heavy and pumps too corporate. They fight the breezy energy of linen. Instead, go for shoes that are light in color and shape. slides, strappy sandals, open toe flats. All of these keep the look balanced. New tones or soft neutrals like tan and beige work especially well. Linen and body shape. Finding your fit. One of the reasons linen feels unflattering is because we treat like it will do the work for us. But with the right cuts and styling tricks, it can absolutely work for any body type. If you're petite, lean into vertical lines. Go for linen pieces that create one long visual column like jumpsuits, wrap dresses, or high-waisted trousers with a fitted top. Avoid oversized shapes unless they're balanced by a cinched waist or structured accessory. If you're curvy, seek out linen with seams, panels, or darts. These give shape to a fabric that otherwise wants to hang straight. Emphasize your waist, balance proportions, and don't be afraid to tailor if something almost fits. If you're tall, you've got room to play. Longer hems, wide leg cuts, dramatic sleeves. Linen responds well to length and movement. Just be sure the fit is intentional so it doesn't slide into wrinkled sack territory. Fabric blends and smarter shopping. Not all linen is created equal. 100% linen is breezy but often stiff and quick to wrinkle. Linen cotton blends hold their shape a little bit better. Linen visos can give you a softer drape. If you want less fuss, look for those blends. And especially if you want your pieces to last longer and wrinkle less. Also, check the thickness. Flimsy linen clings and creases in all the wrong ways. Go for mediumwe linen that holds its shape but still let your skin breathe. Linen isn't a problem. It's our expectations. If you want it to behave like structured wool or polished satin, it won't. But if you meet it where it is and style it accordingly, linen can be one of the most flattering and versatile fabrics in your wardrobe. Let the wrinkles be part of the story, not the whole story. So, next time you reach for a linen piece, don't try to dress it up with stiff, sharp items. Stay cohesive. Keep the look soft, styled, and strategic. And if you want more tips on styling tricky pieces and make them work for your body, make sure to grab the style guide linked below. It's full of practical swabs and refined solutions. Don't forget to subscribe to my channel and thank you for watching.

source_items.body:
Testing the Viral $280 LESET Pants Against a $90 Gap Alternative | Worth the Hype?
I'm pretty sure the last time I shoppedat Gap was when I worked there in 2015. Hi everyone and welcome back to mychannel. I'm going to do a quick pant review today. One of my top wish listitems for the summer was a pair of the poplin Kyoto pants from Lisette. Theyare all over all my social media and I have beenobsessed with them for months. I almost bought them last year and I resisted andthen summer rolled around again and everyone was wearing them and they weresold out. So naturally I wanted them even more than before. The problem is isthat these pants are $280. I feel like there's not a lot of pantsin this world that are worth $280 let alone a poplin pair of pants withkind of limited features. I thought I would run a little bit of experimentbecause these pants have been around for I think 2 years now and because of thatthis summer finally other brands started releasing their version of this pant. SoI picked up a pair from Gap which I have to sayI'm very impressed by. These are also really hard to get. I've noticed thatthey have been sold out on their website consistently for the last couple monthsas well. We're going to do a little side-by-side comparison comparing theGap pants to the Lisette pants. I have both here with me today and I'm going totell you if I think the Lisette ones are actually worth the $280 tag or if thedupes are just as good. So let's start with the Gap pant. So I have oncurrently the Gap poplin double knee barrel pants. They also have just aregular poplin kind of barrel silhouette style pants. Iwas not able to get my hands on those either but I did score with getting onecolor of the double knee style. I think all that it means with the double kneeversus just the regular poplin pant is there is thiskind of panel here and I think it creates more of that horseshoe effectwith the barrel. I read online that these were the closest style to theKyoto pant from Lisette, so that's why I went with these. I bought theseintending to do this side-by-side review thinking that I probably wasn't going tokeep them because like I said, I haven't shopped at Gap in years and I didn'texpect to like them as much as I do. Put them on and I immediately took the tagsoff and I wore them out that night to meet my friends and I received a lot ofcompliments on them as well. So, safe to say I'm kind of obsessed with thesepants. These were $89.95 and I was able to use some type of promocode on the Gap's website. Seems that these pants are excluded from some oftheir major promotions. So, I was only able to get like $10 off. In terms ofsizing, I got these in an extra small because I find that Gap tends to run alittle bit bigger in terms of sizing and I think they fit me perfectly and I didgo ahead and get the petite. I am 5'3 and I'm really glad that I got thepetite. They hit right at my ankle and they really don't overwhelm my frame. Ithink something that can happen a lot with barrel leg styles is when they'retoo long and you're petite, they can really overwhelm your frame. I've beenwearing these non-stop. I've already washed them a few times, so they're alittle little wrinkly cuz I washed them yesterday, but they are just such aneasy throw-on style. I love the tie at the waist. There's also some elastichere. They're stretchy, they're comfortable. I'm not afraid to sit downoutside in these. I'm not afraid to go out to eat and potentially spillsomething on them. I just feel like because they were under $100 and I likethat they're dark, I am not too worried about babying them. I don't know ifanyone else feels that way, but especially with a pant style that is$300, I'm probably going to be nervous to sit down on a bench outside. So,overall, I love the Gap ones, and I hope that they come back in stock because Ithink everybody needs a pair of these. I really want other colors because I'mI've been living in them, and I could use at least one more pair. I reallylike these, and I would recommend for sure. After I received these, I gotthese before I got the Lisette ones in the mail, I was like, "I don't even careabout the Lisette ones anymore because, you know, I have to say, I do reallylike the Lisette ones. There is a lot more fabric, as you can tell, these arenot petite. These only come in one length. They're a little bit longer, andI potentially need to get them tailored if I keep them. So, starting off withsome of the things I like, I find that the waistband is more adjustable onthese. You're able to pull it quite a bit tighterif you want, or you can leave them loose. I found that the Gap ones feel alittle bit more fixed at the waist versus these have a little bit moreadjustment ability going on. This could be nice, especially if you These arecalled the Kyoto pant.
